To: R. Adekanmi
From: PC
Subject: Dashboard audit — before tomorrow's steering

R — careful read of the dashboard. The headline: as drafted, it tells a story neither the CISO nor T. Adeyemi believes any more. The 14-week green run, the corridor comments, and the actual workstream state all point the same way. Below by section, with what I think you should change before tomorrow vs going forward.

1. FOUR-QUESTION FRAMEWORK — DASHBOARD ANSWERS NONE OF THEM CLEANLY

The chapter's four questions: (a) on track for objectives? (b) where is the pressure? (c) what has changed? (d) what does senior attention need to be on?

- (a) Programme completion 68% is rolled up from soft estimates; walking the underlying it's closer to 55%. Doesn't answer the actual objective (go-live Q4).
- (b) Pressure is hidden — Federation is the source of nearly all the real pressure but reads GREEN.
- (c) No trending — every metric is a point value. The CISO can't tell if Federation has been amber-for-nine-weeks or just turned that way.
- (d) No "decisions needed" or "asks of senior" section at all.

If the CISO asks any of these tomorrow, the dashboard won't help. The framing "if your dashboard cannot answer these, it is decoration" applies.

2. METRIC PRINCIPLES — FOUR PROBLEMS, FOUR FIXES

- Activity-as-outcome: "Programme completion 68%", "Milestones 14 of 15 (93%)", "Workshops 24", "SC meetings 3", "Hours burned" — all activity. None answer "are we going to ship Q4?" Replace milestone count with "critical-path milestones X of Y". Replace hours burned with "burn against critical-path scope". Drop workshops + SC counts entirely.
- Lagging hiding leading: "Defect count trending down (22→17→14)" looks good but the test lead is on extended sick leave, sprint velocity dropped 40%, so we're finding fewer because we're running fewer. The leading indicator is test execution rate — that would show the real story. Replace.
- Composition hidden by total: "Open risks 22" — 11 of 22 are now concentrated on Federation critical path, 4 are external-vendor amber-for-nine-weeks. Total is the noise, composition is the signal. Replace with "critical-path risks (composition view): N high, N medium, age-of-amber per item".
- Snapshot without trend: every metric is one point. Add 12-week trend lines for everything that stays. Without trending, no one can interpret 68% or 4.1.

Survey: the 4.1/5 satisfaction is methodologically broken — 38% response rate, senior stakeholders didn't respond, weighted by junior workshop praise. Drop the metric until we have a sample we can defend.

3. AUDIENCE LAYERS — ONE DASHBOARD CANNOT SERVE THREE

The current dashboard is one screen for delivery team, you, and the CISO. None of them think it serves them; none has told you. Per the chapter, three layers:

- Team operational (daily): velocity, burndown, defects, build status, test execution rate, action tracker. Owned by team leads, refreshed daily.
- Project tactical (weekly, YOU): milestone progress, workstream RAGs with rationale, budget-vs-actual by workstream, top risks composition view, upcoming decisions, trending. Owned by you and PMO analyst.
- Executive strategic (weekly or biweekly, CISO + SC): one page. Overall RAG with rationale (honest), top three risks, sponsor decisions needed, go-live confidence. Owned by R., reviewed by PMO Director.

For tomorrow specifically — propose to R. that we build a one-page executive cut tonight, derived from the project view. Same source data; different package per audience. The chapter line: 'translate between dashboards' — divergence between layers erodes trust, but a properly layered view does not.

4. HONEST COLOR — THE 14-WEEK GREEN RUN IS A LIE BY OMISSION

T. Adeyemi's "the green run is making me nervous" and the CISO's "real view of where the programme is" are both already discounting the dashboard. The chapter is direct: a dashboard full of green for twelve weeks is reporting a lie by omission; readers learn to discount everything it says.

Specifically Federation is amber-going-red on every operational signal — vendor delays, 11-risks-on-critical-path concentration, scope creep on the broker integration, lead reporting GREEN for nine weeks because she's emotionally committed to recovery. That's the named pattern: green-because-PM-wants-it-to-be.

Both mechanisms — automatic color-by-threshold AND PM-judgment-override with written rationale. We need both, but tomorrow the override mechanism matters more. Specifically I'd recommend:
- Federation: move to AMBER (or RED — see below) with rationale citing vendor, critical-path-concentration, broker scope.
- Overall RAG: move to AMBER given Federation is on critical path and we are 4 months from go-live.
- Add a "color rationale" column to every workstream so readers can see why the colour is what it is.

The chapter's discomfort test: honest dashboards look slightly uncomfortable. Readers who see a dashboard that reflects real variation and occasional bad news learn to believe the green when it appears. Going honest tomorrow rebuilds trust the dashboard has lost.

On Federation specifically — I'm not sure whether the right call is AMBER or RED. I'd like to walk the underlying numbers with the lead this afternoon and bring you a separate read this evening. RED implies a material change is needed (per Ch 13's definition); AMBER implies exposure with senior awareness appropriate. My instinct is RED but I want the conversation with her first. Either way, you have the final call.

5. COORDINATOR ROLE — WHAT I'M COMMITTING TO

Verify before publishing. Going forward I'll reconcile each workstream lead's reported % against the underlying burn-up and accepted deliverables BEFORE the dashboard refreshes. The 68% vs 55% gap is on me — I refreshed without verifying. That's a process fix I can make this week.

Drop noise, add signal. "Workshops conducted" and "SC meetings" have not been referenced by any audience in 8 weeks. Drop. Add: critical-path-milestones, test execution rate, critical-path risk composition, trending for everything.

Translate between layers. When we build the three-layer split, my job is to keep the source data consistent — if the executive view says X, the project view's underlying numbers reconcile to X. Divergence between layers erodes trust faster than amber/red ever does.

Don't hide behind the dashboard. When the CISO asks tomorrow "what's the real risk?" — the answer is Federation, and that's an answer I should be able to give even if the dashboard doesn't have a chart for it. Pointing at the dashboard instead of answering would be the failure.

On Federation specifically — I want to surface what I'm seeing to you before steering. I won't put Federation on AMBER/RED on the dashboard tomorrow without your sign-off, but the conversation needs to happen tonight, not in steering. Worst case: you and the lead disagree with my read, we hold GREEN, and the CISO surfaces it herself (which she will). Best case: you go in tomorrow having already made the call, with rationale, and the steering becomes a focused recovery conversation rather than a discovery one.

Two things I am NOT certain on and want your read: (a) Federation AMBER vs RED — my walk-through with the lead this afternoon will inform; (b) whether to brief T. Adeyemi separately before steering, given her own concern signal — your relationship with her, your call.

PC
